text: of thought in politics and science could not cope with the situation (Voegelin 1998: 17–28). The period remained a ‘fluid field of sentiments and ideas’, until a new systematic view of man and the world took shape, and this ‘crystallization into systematic form takes place only in the seventeenth century’ (ibid.: 134). Applying the concept of liminality to large-scale societies undergoing particularly stressful periods, it can be suggested that two main dimensions of the crisis of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries were singled out – by single personalities, and by a larger filtering process, here only hinted at – as particularly pressing, and these dimensions would come to be understood as the real reasons of the crisis: there was the problem of knowledge about the world (and the role of bodily perception in the process of knowledge), which would establish itself as the central problem of modern philosophy; and there was the problem of social order, which would become the main preoccupation of rule and of political science. The two problems are indeed typical founding challenges and archetypical themes in liminality, as shown by human experiences of ritual passages. But it is also clear that alternative problematizations could and did in fact exist. 17 Moreover, both formulations would secure, from opposite ends, not only the success of ‘absolutism’, but also the future viability of alternative models. Such alternative models would, paradoxically, be required to solve a problem whose very formulation had come to be taken for granted: how the closed human self as mind could reach knowledge and construct a system from its own grounds, and how the ‘unordered’ and power-thirsty self could find social peace through the rational Sovereign. It was exactly this formulation, this problematization, which became part and parcel of the very problem that it kept producing. Lasting Effects: Orders and Borders of Modern State and Self It is often asked what role the organism analogy has played in the social and political sciences: in what ways does a society resemble (the functioning of) the human person? The question can be inverted: what is the preconceived idea of 17 Voegelin’s sympathy for Jean Bodin as a representative of a ‘Mediterranean modernity’, seen as an alternative to what he termed an ‘Atlantic modernity’ (the Dutch- French-British modernity based on the scientism and secularity of the seventeenth century) must certainly be understood in this way. Bodin recognized the end of the Christian mediaeval worldview and constructed a complete theory of politics. However, Bodin theorized the national state using analogies from a cosmic order and hierarchy, and Bodin’s entire cosmology was still inspired by the richness of the Platonic and Hellenistic traditions, contemplating the order of the soul and its role in the polis, carefully avoiding the materialistic metaphysics of the Hobbesian system (Voegelin discusses Bodin in two different places of his (abandoned) manuscripts that were published in the volume entitled Religion and the Rise of Modernity (1998: 158–68; 180–251), volume V of his History of Political Ideas). It goes without saying that Hobbes read Bodin very selectively. Liminality and the Modern 136 the human person that it can be mapped metaphorically onto state and society? In a Cartesian universe, the individualist self is an autonomous, self-organizing, self-motivated being, who should be able to control the boundaries of his external world well; what modern medical standards would call insanity occurs when he does not know how to handle them. With Descartes this capacity of boundary- drawing became the very possibility of the reason and rationality upon which modernity was epistemologically, scientifically and institutionally built. Divinity enters this self insofar as it follows and respects the truth of logical reason. As suggested above, divinity becomes one with the truth of the self as reasoning mind. Moving our attention to historically shifting concepts of state, it can, along parallel lines, be suggested that differences in state organization may be thought of in terms of the kinds of borders drawn around the polity. Modern states can simply be defined as states with absolute borders within which they exercise absolute power (Thomassen 2005). By such standards, a threat to state borders amounts to a questioning of its reason for being and a declaration of war. From the seventeenth century states became conceptualized as self-organized, self-motivated beings that operate from their own set of rules, and from a self-foundational rationality, fittingly termed reason of state. Divinity enters the political organization of society insofar as it follows and respects the truth of society. Divinity becomes one with the truth of society. It is in this sense that the Hobbesian Sovereign has a clear parallel in the sovereignty of Descartes’ self. It is reason upon fear against reason upon mind. Yet both thinkers postulated a form of first principle from which truths about the world could be generated from obvious premises. Descartes’ system departed from the cogito, while Hobbes’ single axiom was that of self-preservation. The order they bestowed upon the world would have different effects in different domains, rationalizing thought and governance respectively. And yet the Hobbesian reason of state is structurally similar, almost down to the last detail, to the Cartesian reason of self. Historically and conceptually, the sovereign state came into being with the absolute self. The problem became one of safeguarding borders and boundaries, to keep out, and then to concentrate, order and centralize the energies of the interior. Such perceived security and freedom were obtained, for both state and self, through preserving integrity, through avoiding outside interference, and defending and preserving absolute borders (Neack and Knudson 1996). The very possibility of order turned into a question of peace and security of the interior for both state and self, but in that process the quality of that interior was continuously pre-empted. The human need to mediate passions and drives through an experience of transcendence was (ill)recognized as the very source of disorder.
